docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Welcome to your first step into Android game development with Kotlin! Today, we’ll cover the foundational concepts of game development and set up your first project. By the end of this session, you'll understand the game loop mechanism and create a simple SurfaceView to draw on the screen.

docs.title: What is a Game Loop?
docs.text:
The game loop is the core of any game. It’s a programming construct that ensures the game continuously updates the game state and renders graphics on the screen. Here's a simplified flow:

  1. Process Input: Detect user interactions, like taps or swipes.
  2. Update: Change the game state based on logic or physics.
  3. Render: Draw the updated state on the screen.

This loop runs repeatedly, creating a smooth and interactive experience.

docs.title: Game Loop in Pseudocode
docs.text:
while (gameIsRunning):
    processInput()
    updateGameState()
    renderGraphics()

This logic is implemented in Android using a SurfaceView and a thread for rendering.

docs.title: Setting Up Your First Android Game Project
docs.text:
To get started:

  1. Open Android Studio and create a new Empty Activity project.
  2. Configure the project:
  3. Language: Kotlin
  4. Minimum SDK: API 21 (Android 5.0)
  5. Name the project GameBasics.

After the project is created, we'll set up a SurfaceView to handle custom drawing.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Custom View
docs.text:
Follow the steps below:

  1. Create a new Kotlin class named GameView.
  2. Add the following code (refer to the external file GameView.kt).
  3. Update your MainActivity to use the GameView class. See the file MainActivity.kt.

docs.title: Practice Exercise
docs.text: Your task: 1. Modify the GameView class to draw a rectangle that changes position each time you touch the screen. 2. Hints: - Use MotionEvent to detect touch input. - Call invalidate() to trigger a redraw of the view.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Today, you learned: 1. The basics of the game loop. 2. How to set up a custom SurfaceView in Android. 3. How to draw on the screen using the Canvas API.

These concepts are the building blocks for creating games on Android. In the next session, we’ll dive into input handling to make your game interactive.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Android Canvas API
  • SurfaceView Documentation
  • Tutorials on basic game loops in Android.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: In the next chapter, we’ll add interactivity to your game by handling user input, including touch gestures. Stay tuned!

docs.title: Day 2: Adding Interactivity - Handling User Input
Introduction
docs.text: Welcome to Day 2 of your game development journey! Today, we’ll focus on adding interactivity to your game. You’ll learn how to handle user input, such as touch events, and modify your game logic in response.

docs.title: Understanding Touch Events
docs.text:
In Android, user input is handled using MotionEvent. The most common touch actions include: - ACTION_DOWN: The user touches the screen. - ACTION_MOVE: The user moves their finger on the screen. - ACTION_UP: The user lifts their finger off the screen.

By overriding the onTouchEvent method, you can detect and respond to these actions.

docs.title: Task Overview
docs.text: Today, you'll extend your GameView to: 1. Detect touch input. 2. Move a rectangle to the touch position. 3. Redraw the screen with the updated position.

docs.title: Step 1: Modify GameView
docs.text:
1. Add variables to store the rectangle’s position.
  2. Override the onTouchEvent method to capture touch coordinates.
  3. Call invalidate() to trigger a redraw of the SurfaceView.

Refer to the updated code in GameView.kt.

docs.title: Step 2: Visual Feedback
docs.text: Add logic to draw a rectangle at the touch position: - Use the Canvas.drawRect method. - Clear the screen before redrawing by using Canvas.drawColor.

docs.title: Practice Exercise
docs.text:
Modify the program to: 1. Draw multiple rectangles on the screen, keeping track of all touch points. 2. Clear the screen when a double-tap gesture is detected.

Hints: - Use a List<Pair<Float, Float>> to store touch points. - Detect double-tap gestures using GestureDetector.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Today, you learned: 1. How to handle user input using MotionEvent. 2. How to update the game state based on touch events. 3. How to render interactive elements on the screen.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• MotionEvent Documentation
  • GestureDetector Guide
  • Tutorials on handling touch input in Android.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: In the next session, we’ll add more complexity by incorporating physics-based movement. Get ready to see your objects come to life!

docs.title: Day 3: Adding Physics to Your Game
Introduction
docs.text: Today, we’ll bring your game objects to life by incorporating basic physics principles. You’ll learn how to: 1. Simulate movement. 2. Handle boundaries (e.g., screen edges). 3. Add gravity for a realistic effect.

docs.title: Understanding Physics in Games
docs.text:
Physics in games simplifies real-world principles into manageable rules. Core concepts include: - Velocity: Speed in a particular direction. - Gravity: A force pulling objects downward. - Collision Detection: Determining when objects interact.

In this session, we’ll apply these concepts to a bouncing ball simulation.

docs.title: Step 1: Extend GameView
docs.text:
1. Add variables for the ball's position, velocity, and radius.
  2. Update the drawCanvas method to draw a ball at the current position.
  3. Add a game loop to update the ball’s position over time.

Refer to the external code file GameView.kt.

docs.title: Step 2: Handle Screen Boundaries
docs.text: Modify the game logic to: 1. Reverse the ball’s velocity when it hits the screen edges. 2. Gradually reduce velocity to simulate friction.

docs.title: Practice Exercise
docs.text:
Expand the program to: 1. Add a second ball with its own velocity and movement logic. 2. Detect and respond to collisions between the two balls.

Hints: - Use the distance formula to detect collisions:

distance = sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y 2 - y1)^2)
  • Reverse velocities upon collision.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Today, you learned: 1. How to simulate movement using velocity. 2. How to handle boundaries and collisions. 3. How to implement simple physics in your game.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Canvas.drawCircle Documentation
  • Tutorials on physics simulation in Android games.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: In the next session, we’ll enhance the game by incorporating animations for smoother transitions. Get ready for a visually dynamic experience!

docs.title: Day 4: Adding Animations for Smooth Gameplay
Introduction
docs.text: Today, we’ll take your game to the next level by incorporating animations. Smooth animations make games more visually appealing and immersive. You’ll learn how to: 1. Use Android’s ValueAnimator for animations. 2. Transition objects seamlessly across the screen.

docs.title: Why Animations Matter
docs.text:
Animations enhance the user experience by: - Making transitions fluid and natural. - Providing feedback on user actions. - Highlighting important game events.

In game development, animations are essential for creating a dynamic environment.

docs.title: Step 1: Integrate ValueAnimator
docs.text:
1. Use ValueAnimator to create smooth movement transitions.
  2. Replace manual position updates with an interpolated animation.
  3. Synchronize animations with the game loop.

Refer to the GameView.kt file for implementation details.

docs.title: Step 2: Add Custom Interpolators
docs.text: Experiment with custom interpolators to control the speed of animations: - Use AccelerateDecelerateInterpolator for natural movement. - Create a custom interpolator for unique effects.

docs.title: Practice Exercise
docs.text:
Enhance the program to: 1. Animate the ball’s color as it moves across the screen. 2. Introduce a shrinking or growing effect during collisions.

Hints: - Use ArgbEvaluator with ValueAnimator for color transitions. - Update the ball's radius dynamically during animations.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Today, you learned: 1. How to use ValueAnimator for smooth animations. 2. How to customize animations with interpolators. 3. How to synchronize animations with game logic.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• ValueAnimator Documentation
  • Animation Interpolators Guide

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: In the next session, we’ll introduce player interaction mechanics, such as buttons and game menus. Get ready to enhance user engagement!

docs.title: Day 5: Player Interaction and Game Menus
Introduction
docs.text: Interactivity is a cornerstone of any game. Today, we’ll: 1. Introduce buttons and touch interactions. 2. Create a simple main menu to start the game. 3. Implement pause and resume functionality.

docs.title: Why Interactivity Matters
docs.text: Interactivity turns static visuals into an engaging experience. Key components include: - Touch Controls: Allow players to influence the game world. - Menus: Provide intuitive navigation and control. - Game State Management: Pause, resume, and restart gameplay seamlessly.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Main Menu
docs.text:
1. Add a new MenuView class to display buttons (e.g., "Start Game").
  2. Use TextView or Button widgets for menu options.
  3. Switch to the game view when a button is tapped.

Refer to MenuView.kt for implementation details.

docs.title: Step 2: Handle Touch Events in the Game
docs.text:
1. Override the onTouchEvent method in GameView.
  2. Track the player's touch position.
  3. Respond to taps or swipes to move the ball or trigger actions.

docs.title: Step 3: Add Pause and Resume Functionality
docs.text:
1. Introduce a "Pause" button.
  2. Use a flag to control game state (paused or running).
  3. Stop animations and physics updates when paused.

Refer to GameView.kt for implementation details.

docs.title: Practice Exercise
docs.text:
Expand the program to: 1. Add a "Restart Game" button in the menu. 2. Display the player's score when the game is paused. 3. Allow the player to drag the ball using touch gestures.

Hints: - Use onTouchEvent to track touch movements. - Maintain a score variable and update it during gameplay.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Today, you learned: 1. How to create a simple game menu. 2. How to handle touch interactions. 3. How to implement pause, resume, and restart functionality.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• TouchEvent Documentation
  • Tutorials on game menus and interaction design.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: In the next session, we’ll add scoring and introduce obstacles to challenge the player. Get ready to make your game more competitive!
